title: Street Editor Flutter Frontend Project Report

issues.display_title: Issue #83: [Tour Detail] Enhance Flow Checking Panorama From GoThru
issues.main_description_html:
[Tour Detail] Enhance Flow Checking Panorama From GoThru (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: docs/analyze.md §2.7 (publish/upload), §3.3 (panoramas incl. gothru_exist/uploaded), §3.4 (GoThru API). Mirrors the RN behavior where, before/around upload, the app checks each local panorama against GoThru to determine what already exists vs what still needs uploading. Related RN work: street-editor#900 (retry condition based on per-panorama GoThru check) and street-editor#901 (loading caption "Checking panorama {filename}.jpg").

Goal

Enhance the "checking panorama from GoThru" flow in the Flutter app so it reliably and clearly checks each local panorama against GoThru — determining existence/upload state per panorama — with accurate progress feedback and resilient error handling.

Scope

  • For each local panorama, query GoThru to determine whether it already exists (gothru_exist) / is uploaded.
  • Update local state (uploaded / existence flags) to reflect the true server state.
  • Show clear progress while checking, naming the current file (e.g. "Checking panorama {filename}.jpg") and overall count.
  • Resilient handling: continue/skip on a single failed item, surface errors, support cancel/retry; respect connectivity.
  • Result feeds the upload/retry decision (only missing panoramas are queued).

Out of Scope

  • The upload action itself (separate Upload Tour task); the loading-caption visual design (UI task); connections/levels.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Each local panorama is checked against GoThru; local existence/upload flags reflect the result.
  • Progress is shown per panorama (current file + count), not a bare spinner.
  • A single failed check does not abort the whole flow (logged/handled); overall errors are surfaced.
  • Flow is cancellable and retryable; requires connectivity (clear offline handling).
  • The check result correctly drives which panoramas still need uploading (no duplicates).
  • Labels via i18n.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Check all panoramas (happy path)

  1. Open a tour with a mix of already-uploaded and local-only panoramas → run the check.
  2. Expected: Each panorama is checked against GoThru; flags update; progress names each file with a count.

TC-2 — Correct existence detection

  1. After checking, inspect the panoramas.
  2. Expected: Panoramas present on GoThru are flagged as existing; missing ones flagged as needing upload.

TC-3 — Progress feedback

  1. Run the check on a tour with many panoramas.
  2. Expected: "Checking panorama {filename}.jpg" (or equivalent) advances per file; overall count progresses; UI not frozen.

TC-4 — Single item failure resilience

  1. Force one panorama's check to fail (e.g. transient error).
  2. Expected: Flow continues for the rest; the failed item is handled/surfaced; no full abort.

TC-5 — Offline

  1. Run the check offline.
  2. Expected: Clear "no internet" handling; check does not run against GoThru / fails gracefully.

TC-6 — Cancel / retry

  1. Start the check, cancel midway; then retry.
  2. Expected: Cancel stops cleanly; retry re-checks and completes.

TC-7 — Drives upload correctly

  1. After checking, proceed to upload.
  2. Expected: Only panoramas missing on GoThru are queued; no duplicates created server-side.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: the check does not modify panorama images/levels/connections — only existence/upload flags.

🧪 QA Tester Results — Issue #83 · [Tour Detail] Enhance Flow Checking Panorama From GoThru

Street Editor Flutter App:

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.15.06.46.mp4

App GoThru:

2026-06-18.15-06-02.mp4

issues.display_title: Issue #69: [Tour Detail] Fix Integrate Arrow Angle
issues.main_description_html:
[Tour Detail] Fix Integrate Arrow Angle (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: Connection arrows in the panorama viewer are placed by the connection angle (docs/analyze.md §2.2, §3.3 connections.angle). This task fixes incorrect arrow angle/placement so arrows point at the true direction of the linked panorama.

Goal

Fix the arrow angle calculation/rendering in the fullscreen panorama viewer so each connection arrow points in the correct real-world direction toward its linked panorama.

Scope

  • Correct the angle math used to position connection arrows (heading/angle handling).
  • Ensure arrows stay anchored to the correct direction while the user pans.
  • Verify consistency between the stored angle and the rendered direction.

Out of Scope

  • Adding/removing connections; base panorama rendering; zoom/motion.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Arrows point at the actual direction of the connected panorama (matches angle).
  • Arrows remain correctly anchored while panning/looking around.
  • Multiple arrows each point at the correct respective neighbor.
  • No off-by-heading / inverted-direction errors.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Correct direction (happy path)

  1. Open a connected panorama in fullscreen.
  2. Expected: Arrow points toward the real direction of the linked panorama.

TC-2 — Anchoring while panning

  1. Pan the view.
  2. Expected: Arrow stays anchored to its real-world direction (doesn't drift).

TC-3 — Multiple connections

  1. Open a panorama with several connections.
  2. Expected: Each arrow points at the correct neighbor.

TC-4 — Navigate then verify

  1. Tap an arrow → arrive at the neighbor → look back.
  2. Expected: Return arrow points back toward the origin correctly.

TC-5 — Edge angles

  1. Test connections near 0°/180°/360° boundaries.
  2. Expected: No wraparound/inversion errors.

TC-6 — Regression

  1. Compare against the previous buggy behavior.
  2. Expected: Previously wrong angles are now correct; nothing else regressed.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: zoom/motion/navigation still compose correctly with arrows.

issues.display_title: Issue #73: [Street View Account] Integrate Get Account
issues.main_description_html:
[Street View Account] Integrate Get Account (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN Google/Street View account management (src/pages/setting/google-account); Google tokens (google_token/google_access_token); see docs/analyze.md §2.7, §3.4, §2.8.

Goal

Implement "Get Account" for Street View accounts in the Flutter app: fetch and display the Google/Street View account(s) currently linked to the user (used for publishing).

Scope

  • Fetch the linked Street View / Google account(s) from the GoThru/Google API for the current session.
  • Display account identity (name/email/avatar) and which is the default (if multiple).
  • Loading, empty (none linked), and error states.

Out of Scope

  • Add / remove / set-default account (separate tasks); the publish flow.

Acceptance Criteria

  • The linked Street View account(s) are fetched and displayed.
  • Account identity shown; default account indicated if multiple.
  • Empty state when none linked; error state on failure.
  • Works with the signed-in session token.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Get linked account (happy path)

  1. Open Street View Account with at least one linked account.
  2. Expected: The account(s) load and display with identity.

TC-2 — None linked

  1. Open with no linked accounts.
  2. Expected: Clear empty state; option to add (paired task).

TC-3 — Multiple accounts + default

  1. Open with multiple linked accounts.
  2. Expected: All listed; the default is clearly indicated.

TC-4 — Network / API error

  1. Open offline or simulate an error.
  2. Expected: Clear error state with retry; no crash.

TC-5 — Refresh

  1. Re-open / pull-to-refresh.
  2. Expected: List re-fetches current state.

TC-6 — Localization

  1. Switch language.
  2. Expected: Labels/states translated.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: fetching accounts does not alter linkage.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.15.59.mp4

issues.display_title: Issue #74: [Street View Account] Integrate Add Account
issues.main_description_html:
[Street View Account] Integrate Add Account (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN Google/Street View account linking (src/pages/setting/google-account); Google sign-in tokens (google_token/google_access_token); see docs/analyze.md §2.7, §3.4.

Goal

Implement "Add Account" for Street View accounts: let the user link a new Google/Street View account (used for publishing) via the Google auth flow.

Scope

  • "Add account" action → Google sign-in/authorization → link to the user on GoThru.
  • Store/refresh the resulting account in the linked-accounts list.
  • Handle cancel, auth-denied, duplicate (already linked), and network errors.

Out of Scope

  • Get / remove / set-default (separate tasks); the publish flow.

Acceptance Criteria

  • User can link a new Street View / Google account via the auth flow.
  • The new account appears in the linked-accounts list after success.
  • Duplicate add (already-linked account) is handled gracefully.
  • Cancel and auth/network errors are surfaced cleanly.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Add account (happy path)

  1. Street View Account → Add → complete Google auth.
  2. Expected: Account linked; appears in the list.

TC-2 — Cancel auth

  1. Start add, cancel the Google chooser.
  2. Expected: Returns cleanly; nothing added; no error spam.

TC-3 — Duplicate account

  1. Add an account that's already linked.
  2. Expected: Handled gracefully (informs already linked; no duplicate).

TC-4 — Auth denied / insufficient permission

  1. Deny required permissions.
  2. Expected: Clear message; no partial linkage.

TC-5 — Network error

  1. Add while offline / simulate failure.
  2. Expected: Clear error; retry possible.

TC-6 — Persistence

  1. Add an account, relaunch, reopen.
  2. Expected: Linked account persists.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: existing linked accounts unaffected by adding a new one.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.15.59.mp4

issues.display_title: Issue #75: [Street View Account] Integrate Remove Account
issues.main_description_html:
[Street View Account] Integrate Remove Account (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN Google/Street View account management (src/pages/setting/google-account); see docs/analyze.md §2.7, §3.4, §2.8.

Goal

Implement "Remove Account" for Street View accounts: let the user unlink a linked Google/Street View account, with confirmation.

Scope

  • Remove/unlink action on a linked account (with confirm dialog).
  • Call the API to unlink; update the linked-accounts list.
  • Define default handling when the removed account was the default (promote another / clear default).
  • Surface errors; reflect the change immediately.

Out of Scope

  • Get / add / set-default (separate tasks); the publish flow.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Removing an account prompts for confirmation.
  • On confirm: account is unlinked and removed from the list.
  • If the removed account was default, default handling is applied (per agreed rule).
  • Cancel leaves linkage unchanged; errors surfaced; persists after restart.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Remove account (happy path)

  1. Street View Account → remove a linked account → confirm.
  2. Expected: Account unlinked; removed from the list.

TC-2 — Cancel

  1. Tap remove, then cancel.
  2. Expected: Account stays linked.

TC-3 — Remove default account

  1. Remove the account currently set as default.
  2. Expected: Default handling applies (another promoted or default cleared), per the rule.

TC-4 — Network / API error

  1. Remove while offline / simulate failure.
  2. Expected: Clear error; account remains until server confirms.

TC-5 — Persistence

  1. Remove, relaunch, reopen.
  2. Expected: Account stays removed.

TC-6 — Other accounts intact

  1. With multiple accounts, remove one.
  2. Expected: Remaining accounts unaffected.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: removing one account does not break publishing with remaining accounts.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.15.59.mp4

issues.display_title: Issue #76: [Street View Account] Integrate set Default Account
issues.main_description_html:
[Street View Account] Integrate Set Default Account (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN Google/Street View account management (src/pages/setting/google-account); see docs/analyze.md §2.7, §3.4.

Goal

Implement "Set Default Account" for Street View accounts: let the user choose which linked Google/Street View account is the default used for publishing.

Scope

  • Action to mark a linked account as default (single default at a time).
  • Persist the default via the API/local state; update the list to reflect it.
  • Ensure exactly one default; surface errors.

Out of Scope

  • Get / add / remove (separate tasks); the publish flow itself.

Acceptance Criteria

  • User can set any linked account as the default.
  • Exactly one account is default at a time (setting a new one clears the old).
  • The default is clearly indicated in the list and persists across restarts.
  • Errors surfaced; requires connectivity if server-backed.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Set default (happy path)

  1. With multiple linked accounts, set one as default.
  2. Expected: That account is marked default; clearly indicated.

TC-2 — Single default enforced

  1. Set a different account as default.
  2. Expected: The previous default is cleared; only one default remains.

TC-3 — Default persists

  1. Set default, relaunch, reopen.
  2. Expected: The same account remains default.

TC-4 — Single account

  1. With one linked account, view default.
  2. Expected: It is (or can be) the default; no broken state.

TC-5 — Network / API error

  1. Set default offline / simulate failure.
  2. Expected: Clear error; default not changed without confirmation.

TC-6 — Interaction with remove

  1. Set default, then remove that account (paired task).
  2. Expected: Default handling applies per the agreed rule.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: changing the default does not unlink any account.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.15.59.mp4

[Multilanguage] Integrate Multilanguage (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN ships 15 locales via i18n-js (assets/i18n/*.json); see docs/analyze.md §1, §3.1 (i18n). The Flutter app must replicate localization across all supported languages.

Goal

Implement multi-language support in the Flutter app: load localized strings for all supported languages, let the user switch language, and apply it across the UI.

Scope

  • Set up Flutter localization (e.g. flutter_localizations + ARB/JSON resources) covering the 15 supported locales.
  • Migrate the existing translation keys/values from the RN assets/i18n JSON.
  • Language selector in settings; persist the chosen language; apply app-wide immediately.
  • Fallback to a default locale for missing keys.

Out of Scope

  • Adding new languages beyond the existing set; translating new copy not present in RN.

Acceptance Criteria

  • All supported locales load and render correctly.
  • User can switch language; selection persists across restarts.
  • UI updates app-wide on language change (no stale/mixed text).
  • Missing keys fall back to the default locale (no raw keys shown).
  • Existing RN translation content is migrated/available.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Switch language (happy path)

  1. Settings → change language to a non-default locale.
  2. Expected: UI updates to the chosen language across screens.

TC-2 — Persistence

  1. Change language, relaunch the app.
  2. Expected: The selected language is retained.

TC-3 — Coverage spot-check

  1. Navigate key screens in a couple of languages.
  2. Expected: Strings are translated; no raw i18n keys / missing text.

TC-4 — Fallback

  1. Force a missing key (or check an untranslated string).
  2. Expected: Falls back to default locale, not a raw key.

TC-5 — RTL / long strings (if applicable)

  1. Use a language with long words.
  2. Expected: Layouts hold; no clipping/overlap.

TC-6 — Default on first run

  1. Fresh install.
  2. Expected: Sensible default locale (device language or English fallback).

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: switching language does not break navigation or layout.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.24.32.mp4

[Delete Account] Integrate Delete Account (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN account deletion with OTP confirmation (src/pages/setting/delete-account, .../otp); see docs/analyze.md §2.8. (Pairs with the UI redesign task on the design board.)

Goal

Implement the Delete Account flow in the Flutter app: the user confirms intent, verifies via OTP, the account is deleted on GoThru, and the app signs out and clears local data.

Scope

  • Entry point in Settings with a clear warning.
  • Request + verify OTP (request code, enter, resend, expiry/error handling).
  • Call the GoThru delete-account API; on success, sign out and clear local session/data.
  • Surface all outcomes (success, wrong/expired OTP, network error).

Out of Scope

  • The visual redesign (tracked separately on the UI design board).

Acceptance Criteria

  • Delete flow requires explicit confirmation + OTP verification.
  • Successful deletion removes the account server-side, signs out, and clears local data.
  • Wrong/expired OTP and network errors are surfaced with retry/resend.
  • No way to delete without passing OTP; requires connectivity.
  • Labels via i18n.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Delete account (happy path)

  1. Settings → Delete Account → confirm → request OTP → enter valid code.
  2. Expected: Account deleted; app signs out; local data cleared; returns to Login.

TC-2 — Wrong OTP

  1. Enter an incorrect OTP.
  2. Expected: Clear error; deletion not performed; can retry.

TC-3 — Expired OTP + resend

  1. Let the OTP expire, then resend.
  2. Expected: New code works; expired one rejected.

TC-4 — Cancel

  1. Start the flow, then cancel before OTP.
  2. Expected: Account intact; no changes.

TC-5 — Offline

  1. Attempt deletion offline.
  2. Expected: Clear "no internet" message; not started.

TC-6 — Post-delete state

  1. After successful deletion, try to use the app.
  2. Expected: Must sign in again; old session/data gone.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: normal logout/profile flows unaffected.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.31.12.mp4

[App] Create Back Handler Event (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN navigates with React Navigation (native-stack/drawer) with gestureEnabled: false (docs/analyze.md §3.1, src/services/rooter.js). The Flutter app needs a consistent, app-wide back-button / back-gesture handler so hardware back (Android) and back gestures behave predictably across screens — especially during long-running flows (capture, upload, checking) where an accidental back press could interrupt work or lose data.

Goal

Implement a centralized back handler in the Flutter app so the system back action (Android hardware/gesture back, and in-app back) is intercepted and handled consistently per screen — including guards on destructive/in-progress flows and a safe app-exit behavior.

Scope

  • App-wide back-handling mechanism (e.g. PopScope/WillPopScope pattern or a shared wrapper) usable by any screen.
  • Per-screen back behavior:
    • Normal screens: pop to the previous route.
    • Forms/edit screens with unsaved changes: prompt to discard/keep before leaving.
    • Long-running flows (capture, upload, checking panorama): block or confirm before interrupting.
    • Root/Main screen: confirm exit (e.g. "press back again to exit" or a confirm dialog) instead of abruptly closing.
  • Consistent behavior for hardware back, gesture back, and in-app back arrows.

Out of Scope

  • Redesigning navigation structure/routes; individual screen content.

Acceptance Criteria

  • A reusable back-handler mechanism exists and is applied consistently across screens.
  • Hardware/gesture back and in-app back behave the same per screen.
  • Screens with unsaved changes prompt before leaving.
  • In-progress flows (capture/upload/checking) are guarded against accidental back.
  • Back on the root/Main screen does not close the app abruptly (confirm/double-press).
  • No dead-ends or stuck screens; behavior is predictable everywhere.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Standard back (happy path)

  1. Navigate into a screen → press hardware/gesture back.
  2. Expected: Returns to the previous screen as expected.

TC-2 — Unsaved changes guard

  1. Edit a form (e.g. tour/level edit) without saving → press back.
  2. Expected: Prompt to discard/keep; leaving only after the choice.

TC-3 — In-progress flow guard

  1. During capture / upload / checking panorama → press back.
  2. Expected: Back is blocked or asks for confirmation; the flow isn't silently interrupted/corrupted.

TC-4 — Root/Main exit behavior

  1. On the Main screen → press back.
  2. Expected: App does not close abruptly — confirm dialog or double-press-to-exit, per design.

TC-5 — Gesture vs hardware parity

  1. Use the back gesture, then the hardware button, on the same screen.
  2. Expected: Identical behavior for both.

TC-6 — Deep navigation

  1. Navigate several screens deep, then back repeatedly.
  2. Expected: Each back pops one level correctly; no skipped/stuck screens.

TC-7 — Modals / bottom sheets

  1. Open a modal/bottom sheet → press back.
  2. Expected: Back dismisses the modal first (not the underlying screen).

Platforms: Android (hardware/gesture back) + iOS (swipe-back/in-app back).
Regression watch: existing navigation transitions still work; no double-pop or lost routes.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.33.44.mp4

issues.display_title: Issue #70: [Tour Detail] Integrate Panorama Marker On Map
issues.main_description_html:
[Tour Detail] Integrate Panorama Marker On Map (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: RN map view plots panoramas by latlng/heading on a Google map (src/pages/map; see docs/analyze.md §2.5, §3.3 panoramas.latlng/heading).

Goal

Implement panorama markers on the map in the Flutter app: plot each panorama at its location on a map within Tour Detail, so the user can see the tour's spatial layout.

Scope

  • Render a map (Google Maps Flutter) inside Tour Detail.
  • Place a marker per panorama at its latlng; indicate heading where available.
  • Tapping a marker selects/opens that panorama; map fits/centers on the tour.
  • Handle panoramas without coordinates gracefully.

Out of Scope

  • Repositioning panoramas / editing headings on the map (separate concern); connections rendering.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Each located panorama is shown as a marker at its latlng.
  • Map centers/fits to the tour's panoramas.
  • Tapping a marker selects/opens the corresponding panorama.
  • Panoramas without coordinates are handled (excluded/flagged), no crash.
  • Works with the map provider configured; loading/error states handled.

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Markers render (happy path)

  1. Open a tour whose panoramas have coordinates → view map.
  2. Expected: A marker per panorama at the correct location; map fits the set.

TC-2 — Tap marker

  1. Tap a marker.
  2. Expected: The corresponding panorama is selected/opened.

TC-3 — Heading indication

  1. View markers for panoramas with headings.
  2. Expected: Heading is indicated (e.g. arrow/orientation) where available.

TC-4 — Missing coordinates

  1. Open a tour with some panoramas lacking latlng.
  2. Expected: Those are excluded/flagged gracefully; located ones still render.

TC-5 — Many markers

  1. Open a tour with many panoramas.
  2. Expected: Map remains responsive; markers render without freeze.

TC-6 — Offline / map error

  1. View map offline or with a map load failure.
  2. Expected: Clear handling; no crash.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: map view does not modify panorama coordinates/headings.

Demo by Developer

WhatsApp.Video.2026-06-18.at.16.39.14.mp4

issues.display_title: Issue #85: [Listing Detail] Fix Import Connection Panorama From GoThru
issues.main_description_html:
[Listing Detail] Fix Import Connection Panorama From GoThru (Flutter Migration)

Migration context: Port of GoThru 360 Virtual Tour Maker (React Native / Expo) → Flutter.
Source reference: docs/analyze.md §2.2 (connections), §2.6 (listings/import), §3.3 (connections: main_id, link_id, angle, meters; panoramas), §3.4 (listings API). When importing a tour from GoThru (Listing Detail), the connections between panoramas must be imported correctly so the tour graph (walk-through links) matches the server. This task fixes connection import producing missing/wrong/duplicated links.

Goal

Fix the import of panorama connections from GoThru in Listing Detail so that, after importing a tour, the local connections exactly reflect the server's tour graph — correct pairs, angles, and no missing/duplicated edges.

Problem

When importing a tour from GoThru, connections between panoramas are imported incorrectly — symptoms may include: missing connections, links pointing to the wrong panorama, wrong angle/direction, or duplicated edges. As a result the imported tour's navigation graph doesn't match the original on GoThru.

Scope

  • Map server panorama ids to the locally-imported panoramas (e.g. via pano_id) so main_id/link_id resolve to the right local rows.
  • Import each connection with correct angle/meters and project association.
  • Prevent duplicate/self connections; handle connections referencing not-yet-imported panoramas gracefully.
  • Ensure idempotency: re-importing the same tour does not duplicate connections.

Out of Scope

  • The overall import-tour flow (separate task); adding/removing connections manually; arrow rendering.

Acceptance Criteria

  • After import, local connections match the server tour graph (same pairs).
  • Each connection resolves to the correct local panoramas (id mapping via pano_id).
  • angle/meters and direction are preserved/correct.
  • No missing, wrong-target, self, or duplicate connections.
  • Re-import is idempotent (no duplicate edges).

🧪 Test Scenarios (for QA)

TC-1 — Import connections (happy path)

  1. Import a connected tour from GoThru via Listing Detail.
  2. Expected: All connections imported; the local graph matches the server (same links).

TC-2 — Correct id mapping

  1. Inspect imported connections.
  2. Expected: Each main_id/link_id points to the correct local panorama (via pano_id), not a wrong one.

TC-3 — Angle/direction correctness

  1. Open imported panoramas and check connection directions.
  2. Expected: angle/direction matches the original tour (arrows point correctly).

TC-4 — No duplicates / self-links

  1. Review the imported connection set.
  2. Expected: No duplicate edges, no self-connections.

TC-5 — Idempotent re-import

  1. Import the same tour again.
  2. Expected: Connections are not duplicated; graph stays consistent.

TC-6 — Partial / out-of-order panoramas

  1. Import where a connection references a panorama imported later in the sequence.
  2. Expected: Connection resolves correctly once both ends exist; no dangling/dropped links.

TC-7 — Regression vs old behavior

  1. Compare with the previous buggy import.
  2. Expected: Previously missing/wrong connections are now correct; nothing else regressed.

Platforms: Android + iOS
Regression watch: importing connections does not corrupt imported panoramas/levels or other tours.
